unit.name: From 2D image tasks to video classification and clip training
unit.description: Introduces the guest lecture, reviews 2D recognition tasks, defines video as images plus time, and explains why video models are trained on short low-resolution clips.
unit.summary: The lecture starts by positioning video understanding as an extension of image recognition: images support classification, semantic segmentation, detection, and instance segmentation, but the world is not static [ts=103s] [ts=148s]. A video is defined as a sequence of frames with an extra temporal dimension, represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W, so video understanding must extract features from a 4D volume rather than a single image [ts=185s] [ts=205s]. Video classification is introduced as an action-recognition problem such as distinguishing running, swimming, jumping, eating, or standing, using familiar classifiers and losses once useful video features are available [ts=217s] [ts=263s]. The segment emphasizes the data-size problem: uncompressed SD video is about 1.5 GB per minute, HD is about 10 GB per minute, and raw videos cannot be fed directly to GPUs because inputs, weights, and activations would be too large [ts=350s] [ts=374s]. The practical workaround is to shrink videos both temporally and spatially, train on short clips such as 16 frames at 112 x 112 and low FPS, sample many clips as training data, and average clip predictions at test time for long videos [ts=396s] [ts=480s] [ts=496s].

key_points.text: A video is a 2D image sequence with an added temporal dimension, commonly represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W.
Video classification often recognizes actions or activities rather than static object categories.
Uncompressed video is large: roughly 1.5 GB per minute for SD and about 10 GB per minute for 1920 x 1080 HD.
A practical training input can be a short low-FPS, low-resolution clip such as T=16, H=W=112, about 3.2 seconds at 5 fps.
Long-video inference can run the model on sampled clips and average the clip-level predictions.

kp_catalog.name: Video as a 4D tensor
kp_catalog.description: A video is a sequence of image frames with an explicit temporal dimension, represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because video as a 4D tensor formalizes how a video is a sequence of image frames with an explicit temporal dimension, represented as T x 3 x H x W or 3 x T x H x W, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: Clip-based training and test-time averaging
kp_catalog.description: Long videos are handled by training on short low-FPS, low-resolution clips and averaging sampled clip predictions at inference.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because clip-based training and test-time averaging formalizes how long videos are handled by training on short low-FPS, low-resolution clips and averaging sampled clip predictions at inference, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.
